zero_shot.response:
Lisbon has an excellent and varied museum scene. Here are the top choices by category, with one-line reasons to help you pick based on interests:

Art & Design
- Museu Nacional de Arte Antiga (National Museum of Ancient Art) — Portugal’s principal fine arts collection, excellent for European and Portuguese paintings, sculpture and decorative arts.
- Museu Calouste Gulbenkian — World-class mix of European and Islamic art plus a modern wing and peaceful gardens; compact and beautifully displayed.
- Museu Coleção Berardo (Belém Cultural Center) — The best modern and contemporary art in Lisbon, with major 20th‑ and 21st‑century names.

History & Culture
- Museu Nacional do Azulejo (National Tile Museum) — Unique museum dedicated to Portugal’s decorative tiles (azulejos), housed in a stunning former convent.
- Museu Nacional de Arqueologia (National Archaeology Museum) — Rich collection of local and Mediterranean archaeology; good for ancient artifacts and Iberian history.
- Museu de Lisboa — Multiple sites (e.g., Pimenta Palace) that together tell the story of Lisbon’s urban and social history.

Maritime & Exploration
- Museu de Marinha (Navy/Maritime Museum) — One of the best maritime museums in Europe, with ship models, naval artifacts and connections to the Age of Discoveries.
- Padrão dos Descobrimentos / Belém area museums — Nearby monuments and small exhibition spaces about Portuguese exploration.

Contemporary & Special Interest
- MAAT (Museum of Art, Architecture and Technology) — Striking riverside building with contemporary exhibitions around art, architecture and tech; great photo ops.
- Museu do Fado — Small, engaging museum devoted to Portugal’s fado music tradition, with audio exhibits and neighborhood context (Alfama).
- Museu Nacional do Computador e da Educação (Museu da Água at the Mãe d’Água) — Niche but interesting options like the Water Museum (Mãe d’Água) show engineering and urban history.

Science & Natural History
- Museu Nacional de História Natural e da Ciência — Natural history, geology and historical scientific instruments; good for families.

Tips
- Buy combined or timed-entry tickets (Gulbenkian and Belém Cultural Centre have timed entries).
- Belém is a concentration of must-sees (MAAT, Belém Tower, Jerónimos Monastery, Berardo) and worth a half- to full-day.
- Check opening days—many museums are closed one weekday (often Monday).
- If short on time: prioritize Gulbenkian (for a broad great collection), Calouste Berardo (contemporary), Museu Nacional do Azulejo (unique Portuguese art).

If you tell me your interests (classical art, contemporary, maritime history, music, family-friendly), how much time you have, or where you’ll be staying, I can recommend a tailored 1‑ or 2‑day museum itinerary.

zero_shot.response:
Lisbon has an excellent and varied museum scene. Here are the top choices by category, with one-line reasons to help you pick based on interests:

Art & Design
- Museu Nacional de Arte Antiga (National Museum of Ancient Art) — Portugal’s principal fine arts collection, excellent for European and Portuguese paintings, sculpture and decorative arts.
- Museu Calouste Gulbenkian — World-class mix of European and Islamic art plus a modern wing and peaceful gardens; compact and beautifully displayed.
- Museu Coleção Berardo (Belém Cultural Center) — The best modern and contemporary art in Lisbon, with major 20th‑ and 21st‑century names.

History & Culture
- Museu Nacional do Azulejo (National Tile Museum) — Unique museum dedicated to Portugal’s decorative tiles (azulejos), housed in a stunning former convent.
- Museu Nacional de Arqueologia (National Archaeology Museum) — Rich collection of local and Mediterranean archaeology; good for ancient artifacts and Iberian history.
- Museu de Lisboa — Multiple sites (e.g., Pimenta Palace) that together tell the story of Lisbon’s urban and social history.

Maritime & Exploration
- Museu de Marinha (Navy/Maritime Museum) — One of the best maritime museums in Europe, with ship models, naval artifacts and connections to the Age of Discoveries.
- Padrão dos Descobrimentos / Belém area museums — Nearby monuments and small exhibition spaces about Portuguese exploration.

Contemporary & Special Interest
- MAAT (Museum of Art, Architecture and Technology) — Striking riverside building with contemporary exhibitions around art, architecture and tech; great photo ops.
- Museu do Fado — Small, engaging museum devoted to Portugal’s fado music tradition, with audio exhibits and neighborhood context (Alfama).
- Museu Nacional do Computador e da Educação (Museu da Água at the Mãe d’Água) — Niche but interesting options like the Water Museum (Mãe d’Água) show engineering and urban history.

Science & Natural History
- Museu Nacional de História Natural e da Ciência — Natural history, geology and historical scientific instruments; good for families.

Tips
- Buy combined or timed-entry tickets (Gulbenkian and Belém Cultural Centre have timed entries).
- Belém is a concentration of must-sees (MAAT, Belém Tower, Jerónimos Monastery, Berardo) and worth a half- to full-day.
- Check opening days—many museums are closed one weekday (often Monday).
- If short on time: prioritize Gulbenkian (for a broad great collection), Calouste Berardo (contemporary), Museu Nacional do Azulejo (unique Portuguese art).

If you tell me your interests (classical art, contemporary, maritime history, music, family-friendly), how much time you have, or where you’ll be staying, I can recommend a tailored 1‑ or 2‑day museum itinerary.

Great — here’s a clear, practical full-day plan for a museum-focused day in Lisbon using public transport. I assume tomorrow is a weekday; if it’s a weekend or a specific date with altered hours, tell me and I’ll adjust. I also assume you’re starting from a central Lisbon location (Baixa/Chiado/Cais do Sodré). Times are approximate so you can move at your own pace.

Morning
- 09:00 — Breakfast near Baixa/Chiado
  - Grab a pastel de nata and coffee at a nearby café (e.g., Manteigaria or A Brasileira). This area is well connected and a pleasant place to start.

- 09:45 — Walk to Chiado metro (or Rossio/Cais do Sodré if that’s closer) and head to Belém
  - Take tram 15E from Praça do Comércio or Cais do Sodré toward Belém. Tram 15E is direct and scenic; alternatively take bus 728/714/727 depending on where you are. Journey time ~20–30 minutes.

- 10:20 – 12:00 — Belém Cultural Cluster
  - Museu Nacional dos Coches (Coach Museum) — outstanding collection of royal coaches; allow ~45–60 min.
  - If you prefer contemporary design, choose Museu Coleção Berardo (in the Belém Cultural Center) instead — modern and contemporary art; allow ~60–90 min.
  - Note: these two are near each other; you can visit one in depth or split time between both if you move briskly.
  - Tip: buy tickets online if available to skip queues.

Lunch
- 12:15 — Lunch in Belém
  - Options: café inside Centro Cultural de Belém, or small restaurants around the riverside. Try the original Pastéis de Belém pastry shop for a takeaway tart (it’s very popular so expect a line; there’s seating nearby).

Afternoon
- 13:30 — Return to central Lisbon
  - Take tram 15E back to Cais do Sodré or Praça do Comércio; ~25–30 minutes.

- 14:15 — Museu Nacional de Arte Antiga (National Museum of Ancient Art)
  - From Cais do Sodré, take bus 110/714/732 or a short taxi/ride if you prefer; alternatively take the metro (Baixa-Chiado then a short bus/walk). Allow 1.5–2 hours to see the highlights: Portuguese paintings, European masters, decorative arts.
  - Note opening hours: usually open until 18:00; confirm tomorrow’s schedule.

- 16:30 — MAAT (Museum of Art, Architecture and Technology) or Museu do Fado (pick based on interest)
  - MAAT: located along the river near Belém, contemporary exhibitions and striking architecture. From central Lisbon you can take tram 15E again or catch the train from Cais do Sodré to Belém. Allocate ~60–90 min.
  - Museu do Fado: in Alfama area, dedicated to Portuguese fado music history; easier to combine if you want to explore Alfama’s streets. From central Lisbon take tram 28E or walk from Baixa. Allocate ~45–60 min.
  - Recommendation: if you enjoyed Belém earlier and want more contemporary art/architecture, choose MAAT; if you prefer cultural history and a stroll in Alfama, choose Museu do Fado.

Late afternoon / Early evening
- 18:00 — Sunset stroll and viewpoint
  - If you’re in Alfama after the Fado museum, walk up to Miradouro de Santa Luzia or Portas do Sol for views over the river.
  - If you’re at MAAT/Belém, walk along the river promenade toward the Tower of Belém for sunset views.

- 19:00 — Dinner
  - Return to central Lisbon (Cais do Sodré, Chiado, or Bairro Alto) by tram/train. Dine in Bairro Alto or Chiado for traditional Portuguese fare or petiscos (Portuguese small plates).
  - If you’re in Alfama, choose a cozy local restaurant and consider an evening fado show (many small houses offer dinner+show).

Transport tips
- Use a Viva Viagem rechargeable card for trams, buses, metro, and trains. You can buy it at metro stations or Cais do Sodré station. A daily/24-hour pass (Carris/Metro) might be cost-effective if you plan many trips; check current fares.
- Tram 28E is iconic but crowded — pickpocket-aware and lines can be long; if you want a less touristy ride, use tram 15E for Belém.
- Check real-time schedules via Google Maps or the Carris/Metro apps. Allow extra time for waiting at stops.

Practical details & quick checklist
- Opening hours: confirm museums open tomorrow and reserve tickets online where possible (Museu Nacional dos Coches, Berardo, MAAT, National Museum of Ancient Art have online info).
- Wear comfortable shoes — Lisbon is hilly and cobbled.
- Bring a small umbrella or light jacket (weather can change quickly by the river).
- Keep your phone charged for maps and timetables; pack a power bank if needed.

If you tell me:
- your exact starting location,
- which museums you’d prefer (classical art, modern art, design, history, music),
- and what time you want to end the day,
I’ll make a minute-by-minute plan with exact public-transport lines and departure times for tomorrow.

zero_shot.response:
Here is a **museum-focused itinerary** using Lisbon’s excellent public transport. I’ve designed it to minimize backtracking while covering distinct collections (ancient, decorative arts, and cultural heritage).

**⚠️ Important:** Check what day "tomorrow" is—several top museums are closed on **Mondays** (Ancient Art, MAAT) and **Tuesdays** (Gulbenkian, Tile Museum, Fado Museum). I’ve noted alternatives below.

---

### **Pre-Departure: Get Your Transport Pass**
At any Metro station, buy a **Viva Viagem** card (€0.50) and load it with a **24-hour pass (€6.60)**. This covers all metro lines, trams, buses, and the funiculars you’ll need today.

---

### **Morning: The Gulbenkian Foundation** *(Closed Tuesdays)*
**9:30 AM – 12:30 PM**

*   **Getting there:** Metro Blue or Red line to **São Sebastião**. Follow signs for "Museu Calouste Gulbenkian" (10-min walk through the park).
*   **The Museum:** One of Europe’s finest private collections. Start with the **Founder’s Collection** (Egyptian, Greco-Roman, Islamic, Chinese porcelain, and Impressionist paintings including Renoir and Monet). If time permits, see the **Modern Collection** in the separate building next door (20th-century Portuguese art).
*   **Coffee:** The museum café has excellent pastries and garden views.

**Tuesday Alternative:** Head to **Belém** instead. Take tram **15E** from Cais do Sodré to **Museu dos Coches** (Coach Museum) at 10:00 AM, then walk 5 minutes to the **Berardo Collection Museum** (free entry, modern art from Warhol to Picasso).

---

### **Lunch: 12:45 PM – 2:00 PM**
*   **Option A (Near Gulbenkian):** Walk to **Avenida de Roma** or **Avenida de Berna** for traditional Portuguese lunch at **Cervejaria Ribadouro** (seafood) or **O Talho** (modern Portuguese).
*   **Option B (On the move):** Take the Metro to **Cais do Sodré** and grab a quick *prego* (steak sandwich) at **Papo Cheio** before the afternoon museum.

---

### **Afternoon: National Museum of Ancient Art** *(Closed Mondays)*
**2:15 PM – 4:30 PM**

*   **Getting there:** From Cais do Sodré, take **Bus 714** or **Tram 15E** (direction: Algés) and get off at **Rua das Janelas Verdes**. It’s a 2-minute walk.
*   **The Museum:** Housed in a 17th-century palace. Highlights include the **Panels of St. Vincent** by Nuno Gonçalves (Portugal’s "Mona Lisa"), Japanese screens showing Portuguese traders (Nanban art), and works by Hieronymus Bosch, Dürer, and Raphael.
*   **Bonus:** The museum café has a terrace with stunning views over the Tagus River—perfect for a mid-afternoon coffee.

**Monday Alternative:** Take the Metro to **Oriente** (Red line) and visit the **Oceanário** (aquarium) or the **Knowledge Pavilion** (interactive science museum), or take the train from Cais do Sodré to **Cascais** (30 mins) for the small but excellent **Paula Rego House of Stories** (closed Wed).

---

### **Late Afternoon: The Tile Museum** *(Closed Tuesdays)* OR **Fado Museum** *(Closed Tuesdays)*
**5:00 PM – 6:30 PM** *(Choose one based on interest)*

**Option A: National Tile Museum (Museu do Azulejo)** – *For decorative arts lovers*
*   **Getting there:** From Ancient Art, take **Bus 728** from Santos to **Santa Apolónia** station, then **Bus 794** (or walk 15 mins uphill) to the museum.
*   **The Experience:** Set in a beautiful convent (Madre de Deus), this traces 500 years of Portuguese tile history from Moorish patterns to contemporary street art. The gilded chapel alone is worth the trip.

**Option B: Fado Museum** – *For music/culture lovers*
*   **Getting there:** From Ancient Art, take **Tram 25** or **Tram 28** (crowded but scenic) from the Santos stop up to **Alfama** (get off at Largo do Chafariz). Walk 5 minutes to the museum.
*   **The Experience:** Interactive exhibits on Portugal’s soulful music genre. You can listen to recordings from the 1920s and see guitars and costumes. Located in the heart of Alfama.

---

### **Evening: Dinner & Fado**
**7:30 PM onwards**

*   If you chose the **Fado Museum**, you’re already in Alfama. Stay for dinner at **Clube de Fado** (upscale, reservations needed) or **Parreirinha de Alfama** (traditional) with live Fado performances.
*   If you visited the **Tile Museum**, take Bus 794 back to Santa Apolónia and walk 10 minutes into Alfama for dinner, or head to the **Mouraria** neighborhood (Metro: Martim Moniz) for authentic Fado in taverns like **Tasca do Chico**.

---

### **Summary of Transport Used**
1.  **Metro:** São Sebastião, Cais do Sodré, Oriente (if using alternatives).
2.  **Tram 15E:** Central Lisbon to Belém (if using Tuesday alternative) or to Ancient Art.
3.  **Tram 25/28:** Santos to Alfama (steep hills—don’t walk this!).
4.  **Bus:** 714, 728, 794 for specific museum stops.

**Pro Tip:** If you plan to enter 3+ paid museums, buy the **Lisboa Card** at the airport or tourist offices (€22 for 24h). It includes all public transport and free entry to Gulbenkian, Ancient Art, Tile Museum, and the Coach Museum, plus skip-the-line perks.

[search_history_culture] returned:
📚 **Wikipédia: Madrid Metro**
🔗 URL: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Madrid_Metro

The Madrid Metro (Spanish: Metro de Madrid) is a rapid transit system serving the metropolitan area of Madrid, the capital of Spain. First opened in 1919, the system has regularly undergone numerous extensions over the next century, bringing it to today's network which comprises thirteen "conventional" (heavy-rail) lines and three light-rail lines known as Metro Ligero. The network is largely managed by Metro de Madrid S.A., which is owned by the Government of the Community of Madrid.
As of September 2025, the conventional lines have a combined length of 296.6 kilometres (184.3 mi), making Madrid's Metro system the 20th longest rapid transit system in the world, the 6th longest in the world outside of China, and the 3rd longest in Europe behind only the Moscow Metro and the London Underground; the three light-rail lines add a further 27.8 kilometres (17.3 mi) to the total length of the network. The whole system transported a total of 715 million passengers in 2024 – in this metric Madrid's system stands as the 29th most-used in the world and the 5th most-used in Europe, behind Moscow, Paris, London and Istanbul.
Unlike Spanish road traffic and most railway lines in the country, which drive on the right, trains on the heavy-rail lines of the Madrid Metro have operated with left-hand running since the system's inception. The light-rail part of the network, on the other hand, runs on the right, as much of it operates at street level.
Within the Community of Madrid, the Madrid Metro is complemented by the Madrid Cercanías suburban rail services managed by the national rail operator Renfe, as well as a large network of urban bus lines operated by the city-owned EMT Madrid, and a collection of interurban bus lines that serve municipalities outside of the city's boundaries, managed by the Community of Madrid itself but operated by private companies on a concession basis. All of these public transportation networks have a unified fare system, which is managed by the Consorcio Regional de Transportes de Madrid (CRTM), a public body run by a consortium of various local governments of the Community of Madrid.
Trains are in circulation every day between 06:00 and 01:30. There had been plans to extend the operating hours on Friday and Saturday nights – first to 02:30 from 2020, and then to 24-hour operation from 2023 – but the coronavirus pandemic halted these plans, and as of February 2026 the system still closes at 01:30 throughout the week. However, the Metro has been known to stay open for 24 hours on at least two special occasions in its history: in summer 2017 during World Pride and in January 2021 during the Madrid snowstorm.
As of September 2025, the Madrid Metro has 1,709 escalators and 571 lifts.

Great — here’s a clear, practical full-day plan for a museum-focused day in Lisbon using public transport. I assume tomorrow is a weekday; if it’s a weekend or a specific date with altered hours, tell me and I’ll adjust. I also assume you’re starting from a central Lisbon location (Baixa/Chiado/Cais do Sodré). Times are approximate so you can move at your own pace.

Morning
- 09:00 — Breakfast near Baixa/Chiado
  - Grab a pastel de nata and coffee at a nearby café (e.g., Manteigaria or A Brasileira). This area is well connected and a pleasant place to start.

- 09:45 — Walk to Chiado metro (or Rossio/Cais do Sodré if that’s closer) and head to Belém
  - Take tram 15E from Praça do Comércio or Cais do Sodré toward Belém. Tram 15E is direct and scenic; alternatively take bus 728/714/727 depending on where you are. Journey time ~20–30 minutes.

- 10:20 – 12:00 — Belém Cultural Cluster
  - Museu Nacional dos Coches (Coach Museum) — outstanding collection of royal coaches; allow ~45–60 min.
  - If you prefer contemporary design, choose Museu Coleção Berardo (in the Belém Cultural Center) instead — modern and contemporary art; allow ~60–90 min.
  - Note: these two are near each other; you can visit one in depth or split time between both if you move briskly.
  - Tip: buy tickets online if available to skip queues.

Lunch
- 12:15 — Lunch in Belém
  - Options: café inside Centro Cultural de Belém, or small restaurants around the riverside. Try the original Pastéis de Belém pastry shop for a takeaway tart (it’s very popular so expect a line; there’s seating nearby).

Afternoon
- 13:30 — Return to central Lisbon
  - Take tram 15E back to Cais do Sodré or Praça do Comércio; ~25–30 minutes.

- 14:15 — Museu Nacional de Arte Antiga (National Museum of Ancient Art)
  - From Cais do Sodré, take bus 110/714/732 or a short taxi/ride if you prefer; alternatively take the metro (Baixa-Chiado then a short bus/walk). Allow 1.5–2 hours to see the highlights: Portuguese paintings, European masters, decorative arts.
  - Note opening hours: usually open until 18:00; confirm tomorrow’s schedule.
